title: Exposure Calculations and Geometric Principles Review
narrationScript: A Level Two radiographer owns the technique. When the source decays, when the job will not allow the distance the sheet calls for, when the interpreter wants more density, you are the one who adjusts the numbers, and you need to be right the first time, because every retake costs film, schedule, and dose. This lesson reviews the exposure arithmetic and the geometry that make those adjustments routine. Exposure is a product. With an x ray machine, it is milliamperes times minutes. With a gamma source, it is curies times hours. Within wide limits, film with lead screens cares only about the product, not the pieces. Ten milliamperes for two minutes equals twenty milliampere minutes, and so does five milliamperes for four minutes. That trade is called reciprocity, and it is what makes techniques portable. One caution. Fluorescent intensifying screens do not hold reciprocity over long, low rate exposures, so corrections under fluorescent screens need confirmation against a chart or a trial film. And remember that the product only sets how much radiation arrives. The kilovoltage or the isotope sets what kind, and that choice controls penetration and contrast. Scaling a technique never substitutes for choosing the right energy. An exposure chart is a promise with conditions. It says, for this machine, this film system, these screens, this source to film distance, this material, developed to this density, here is the exposure for every thickness at every kilovoltage. Inside those conditions, trust it. Outside them, correct it. Distance changes go by the square of the ratio. Activity and current changes go inversely. Film changes go by the published film factor. Density changes ride the characteristic curve, and a useful working rule is that doubling the exposure raises density by roughly one step on the curve. For gamma work, the chart becomes output arithmetic. Iridium one ninety two delivers roughly half a roentgen per hour per curie at one meter. Cobalt sixty delivers roughly one point three. From output, distance, and the exposure the film needs, you compute time. Here is the discipline for stacked corrections. Work one factor at a time, then multiply. Say the sheet calls for eight minutes with sixty curies at thirty inches. Today the source reads thirty curies and clearance forces you out to forty inches. Decay first. Sixty over thirty is two, so sixteen minutes. Distance next. Forty over thirty, squared, is about one point seven eight. Sixteen times one point seven eight is about twenty eight and a half minutes. If the customer also wants more density, read that factor off the film curve and multiply again. Write every factor on the technique sheet. The most common field error is treating a distance change as linear. A move from thirty to forty inches is a factor of one point seven eight, not one point three three. Different materials enter the same arithmetic through equivalence factors. Copper at iridium energies behaves like about one point one times its thickness in steel. Aluminum behaves like about a third. Multiply the real thickness by the factor, then use the steel chart or table as usual. Now geometry. A source has size, so every edge in the part casts a penumbra, a band where the shadow fades instead of switching cleanly. That band is geometric unsharpness. U G equals F times t over d, where F is the source or focal spot size, t is the distance from the source side of the part to the film, and d is the distance from the source to the part. A source of fifteen hundredths of an inch, a part contributing an inch and a half, and a two foot standoff give about nine thousandths of an inch of unsharpness. Codes cap unsharpness by thickness, commonly around twenty thousandths of an inch for thinner sections, so flip the formula and solve for the minimum distance before you compute the time. Three levers make a sharper image. A smaller source. A longer distance. And film tight against the part, because every gap is unsharpness you chose. Finally, alignment. The image is always slightly magnified, and it distorts whenever the beam, the part, and the film fall out of square, stretching features or shifting them against surface landmarks. Aim the central ray at the center of the area of interest, keep the film parallel and in contact, and angle the beam deliberately only when the technique calls for it, the way an elliptical small bore shot does. Geometry is decided in the first five minutes of setup and read for the life of the film. Set it with intention.
imagePrompt: Two radiographers stretch a tape measure from the collimator on a gamma-ray projector guide tube to a film cassette strapped against a steel pipe weld in a fabrication yard, one recording numbers on a clipboard.

sections.heading: Exposure Is a Product
sections.body:
Film responds to total exposure: intensity multiplied by time. With an X-ray machine that product is **milliampere-minutes (mA·min)**; with a gamma source it is **curie-hours (Ci·hr)**. Within wide limits, film exposed with lead screens obeys **reciprocity**: only the product matters. Ten milliamperes for two minutes, five for four, twenty for one - all 20 mA·min, all the same density, other things equal. That single fact makes techniques portable: a chart built at one current converts to any other by simple ratio, and a gamma technique written for 80 curies converts to today's 43 curies the same way.

Two cautions. **Fluorescent intensifying screens break reciprocity** - very long, low-rate exposures lose effect - so corrections under fluorescent screens need chart or trial-film confirmation. And reciprocity says nothing about *quality*: kilovoltage or isotope choice sets what kind of radiation crosses the part; the product only sets how much. Changing kV changes penetration and contrast - that is a different technique, not a scaled one.

sections.heading: Exposure Charts: A Promise With Conditions
sections.body:
An exposure chart says: *for this machine, this film system, these screens, this source-to-film distance, this material, developed to this density - here is the exposure for each thickness at each kilovoltage.* Inside those conditions it is authoritative. Outside them it must be corrected.

Reading one is mechanical: enter at the part's **steel-equivalent thickness**, rise to the chosen kV curve, read the exposure in mA·min. Choosing the kV is the judgment call: lower kV buys subject contrast at the cost of longer exposures and less latitude; higher kV the reverse. Most shops work the middle of the chart, where the curves are well-proven and times are practical.

Gamma work replaces the chart with **output arithmetic**. Rules of thumb: Ir-192 emits roughly 0.5 R/h per curie at 1 m; Co-60 roughly 1.3 R/h per curie at 1 m (approximate - source construction matters). From output, distance, and the exposure the film system needs, compute time; gamma exposure slide rules and shop tables are built on exactly this arithmetic.

When any chart condition changes, correct by ratio: **distance** by the square, **activity or current** inversely, **film system** by the published film factor, **density** via the characteristic curve. Every correction is a multiplication - stack them in any order, but apply each exactly once.

sections.caption: A typical X-ray exposure chart: every curve holds film system, density, screens, and distance constant - read exposure at the intersection of thickness and kilovoltage.

sections.heading: The Big Three Corrections: Distance, Decay, Density
sections.body:
Three corrections cover most field adjustments. Work them one at a time, then multiply.

- **Decay** - time scales inversely with activity: t₂ = t₁ × (A₁/A₂). Use the halving table, not guesswork.
- **Distance** - time scales with the square of the distance ratio: t₂ = t₁ × (d₂/d₁)².
- **Density** - on the straight-line region of the film's characteristic curve, density rises with exposure; as a working rule, **doubling the exposure raises density by roughly one curve step**. Verify against your film's curve - gradients differ by film class and density level.

Worked example, all three at once. The sheet: 8 minutes, 60 Ci Ir-192, 30-in. SFD, density 2.0. Today: the source reads 30 Ci, clearance forces 40 in., and the customer wants density near 2.5.

- Decay: 8 × (60/30) = 16 min
- Distance: 16 × (40/30)² = 16 × 1.78 ≈ 28.5 min
- Density: this film's curve shows about 1.4× exposure to move 2.0 to 2.5, so 28.5 × 1.4 ≈ 40 min

Shoot 40 minutes. Notice the discipline: each factor written separately, each applied once, the chain recorded on the technique sheet so the next crew inherits arithmetic, not folklore. The single most common field error is applying a distance change linearly - a 40/30 move is a factor of **1.78**, not 1.33.

sections.caption: Approximate radiographic equivalence factors (steel = 1.0): multiply actual thickness by the factor to enter a steel chart - values are energy-dependent approximations, so confirm against your procedure.

sections.heading: Geometric Unsharpness: Ug = F·t/d
sections.body:
A source has size, so every edge in the part casts a **penumbra** - a band where the shadow fades instead of switching cleanly. That band is geometric unsharpness:

**Ug = F × t / d**

- **F** - effective source or focal-spot size, from the source certificate or tube rating (gamma pellet stacks commonly run 0.1-0.2 in.)
- **t** - distance from the **source side of the part** to the film. Measure from the source side because the feature farthest from the film is the worst case.
- **d** - distance from the source to the source side of the part

Example: F = 0.15 in., t = 1.5 in., d = 24 in. gives Ug = 0.15 × 1.5 / 24 ≈ 0.009 in.

Codes cap Ug by material thickness - in the style of codes such as ASME Section V, commonly about 0.020 in. for material under 2 in., 0.030 in. for 2-3 in., and larger above. The cap drives **minimum distance**: rearrange to d ≥ F × t / Ug(max). With F = 0.25 in. and t = 2 in. against a 0.020-in. cap, the source must stand at least 25 in. off the part - no matter how attractive a shorter exposure looks. Check Ug at technique time, not after the film is washed.

sections.caption: Rays from opposite edges of a finite source straddle each object edge, producing the penumbra Ug = F·t/d on the film.

sections.title: Three levers for a sharper image
sections.body:
- **Smaller F** - choose the smaller focal spot or source when output allows.
- **Longer d** - distance is the lever you usually control; doubling d halves Ug and quadruples time - the eternal trade.
- **Smaller t** - film tight to the part; every standoff gap is unsharpness you chose.

When a Ug cap binds, solve d ≥ F·t/Ug(max) **before** computing exposure time.

sections.heading: Magnification, Distortion, and Alignment
sections.body:
Because the film can never sit inside the part, every image is slightly **magnified**: M = SFD/SOD (source-to-film over source-to-object distance). At ordinary SFDs the enlargement is small; it grows quickly when the film stands off or the source crowds the part.

**Distortion** is misrepresented shape or position. It appears when the central beam is not perpendicular to the part and film, or when the film is not parallel to the part: features elongate, foreshorten, or shift relative to surface landmarks. Some distortion is deliberate - the small-bore **elliptical technique** offsets the source so the two wall images of a weld separate into a readable ellipse - but unplanned distortion misplaces indications and can hide planar ones entirely.

Alignment rules worth restating at Level II: aim the central ray at the center of the area of interest; keep the film parallel to the part and in contact; and orient the beam along suspected planar discontinuities, because a lack-of-fusion plane angled away from the beam can vanish from the film. Geometry is decided in the first five minutes of setup and read for the life of the radiograph - set it with intention.

interactive.title: Technique Parameter Solver
interactive.intro: A pipe-rack technique sheet reads: Ir-192, 80 curies, 24-inch SFD, 10-minute exposure, 2-inch steel, target density 2.5. Today the same source measures 40 curies, and an obstruction forces the SFD out to 36 inches. Work the corrections in order, the way you would write them on the sheet.

steps.prompt: First, correct for decay alone. The source has fallen from 80 to 40 curies. What is the new base time?
steps.feedback: Activity halved, so exposure time doubles: 10 × (80/40) = 20 minutes. Decay corrections are pure ratio - the halving table hands you the factor.

steps.prompt: Now layer on the distance change from 24 to 36 inches. What is the corrected time?
steps.feedback: Time scales with the square of the distance ratio: 20 × (36/24)² = 20 × 2.25 = 45 minutes. Corrections multiply - never average them, and never apply a distance change linearly.

steps.prompt: The shot comes back at density 1.25, and the film's straight line shows it received about half the exposure needed for density 2.5. The interpreter needs a retake at full density. New time?
steps.feedback: On the straight-line region density tracks exposure, and the curve says the film got half what it needed - so double the 45 minutes to 90. Confirm density moves against your film's characteristic curve, not by feel.

steps.prompt: Before shooting, verify geometry. The source pellet measures 0.12 inch and the 2-inch weld has the film in contact. At the 36-inch SFD, does the setup meet a 0.020-inch Ug limit?
options: Only if the kilovoltage is reduced
Cannot be determined without the film class
steps.feedback: Source-to-object distance d = 36 − 2 = 34 in., so Ug = 0.12 × 2 / 34 ≈ 0.007 in. - comfortably inside a 0.020-in. cap. Energy and film class have no effect on geometric unsharpness.

checkQuestions.question: An exposure chart built at 10 mA calls for 12 minutes on your part. The aging tube now limits you to 6 mA. What exposure time keeps the same film density?
checkQuestions.explanation: The chart demands 10 × 12 = 120 mA·min. By reciprocity, 120 ÷ 6 mA = 20 minutes delivers the same exposure product.

checkQuestions.question: A 6-minute gamma exposure at a 30-inch SFD must be reshot at 60 inches. What is the new time?
checkQuestions.explanation: Time scales with the square of the distance ratio: (60/30)² = 4, and 6 × 4 = 24 minutes.

checkQuestions.question: A 5-minute technique was written when the Ir-192 source held 60 curies at a 48-inch SFD. Today the source reads 30 curies, but you are permitted to shoot at 24 inches. What is the new time?
checkQuestions.explanation: Decay doubles the time (60/30 = 2); halving the distance cuts it to one quarter ((24/48)² = 0.25). Net: 5 × 2 × 0.25 = 2.5 minutes.

checkQuestions.question: With an effective source size of 0.2 in., a source-to-object distance of 20 in., and a feature-to-film distance of 1 in., the geometric unsharpness is:
checkQuestions.explanation: Ug = F × t / d = 0.2 × 1 / 20 = 0.010 in. - inside a typical 0.020-in. cap for thin material.

checkQuestions.question: Using approximate equivalence factors at Ir-192 energies (copper ≈ 1.1), a 2.0-inch copper section should be exposed like about how much steel?
checkQuestions.explanation: Multiply actual thickness by the factor: 2.0 × 1.1 = 2.2 in. of steel equivalent, then enter the steel exposure data at that thickness.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course, FQ 1.3 (exposure calculations)
CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Film Quality and Manufacturing Processes Course, FQ 1.4 (geometric exposure principles)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2: geometric unsharpness limits and technique requirements (general reference)
Published radiographic equivalence factor tables and gamma source output constants (approximate values, general reference)
